perturbation.original_text: Payment shall be made within 30 days from the receipt of invoice, and following the invoice verification by Organiser.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment shall be made within 90 days from the receipt of invoice, and following the invoice verification by Organiser.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment term from 30 days to 90 days could be problematic under German law if it constitutes an unfair disadvantage to the Mentor (creditor).
perturbation.law_explanation: § 271 of the BGB dictates performance immediately unless otherwise agreed. While parties can agree to different payment terms, excessively long payment periods (like 90 days in this modified version) may be considered unfair, especially for smaller contractors, and thus may violate general principles of good faith and fair dealing implied under German law, potentially rendering that term unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 271 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 271 Leistungszeit (1) Ist eine Zeit für die Leistung weder bestimmt noch aus den Umständen zu entnehmen, so kann der Gläubiger die Leistung sofort verlangen, der Schuldner sie sofort bewirken. (2) Ist eine Zeit bestimmt, so ist im Zweifel anzunehmen, dass der Gläubiger die Leistung nicht vor dieser Zeit verlangen, der Schuldner aber sie vorher bewirken kann. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The change from 30 to 90 days for payment, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation, potentially creates an unfair disadvantage to the Mentor, which could be problematic under German law. The Law Explanation references § 271 of the BGB, indicating that performance is expected immediately unless otherwise agreed. While agreements for different payment terms are possible, excessively long periods may violate principles of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both contain the text of § 271 BGB, which states that if a time for performance is not specified, the creditor can demand performance immediately. Although the contract specifies a time, the extension to 90 days could be deemed 'excessively long' and thus an unfair deviation from the immediate performance principle, potentially contradicting the spirit of § 271, and thus good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Mentor shall require his/her 5 employees who will have access to Confidential Information to commit to a non -disclosure agreement that protects the Confidential Information to at least the same degree as this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Mentor shall request his/her employees who will have access to Confidential Information to consider signing a non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: German law requires employers to take reasonable steps to protect trade secrets. The original text mandates a NDA, but the changed text weakens the obligation by only requesting employees to 'consider signing' a NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 4 of the German Trade Secrets Act requires trade secret holders to take reasonable measures to keep their trade secrets secret. Changing the obligation from requiring employees to sign an NDA to just 'considering' it weakens the security measures significantly and may violate the employer's duty under the act. This is especially true if the mentor doesn't take other measures to protect confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 4 GeschGehG - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Gesetz zum Schutz von Geschäftsgeheimnissen (GeschGehG) § 4 Handlungsverbote (1) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangt werden durch 1. unbefugten Zugang zu, unbefugte Aneignung oder unbefugtes Kopieren von Dokumenten, Gegenständen, Materialien, Stoffen oder elektronischen Dateien, die der rechtmäßigen Kontrolle des Inhabers des Geschäftsgeheimnisses unterliegen und die das Geschäftsgeheimnis enthalten oder aus denen sich das Geschäftsgeheimnis ableiten lässt, oder 2. jedes sonstige Verhalten, das unter den jeweiligen Umständen nicht dem Grundsatz von Treu und Glauben unter Berücksichtigung der anständigen Marktgepflogenheit entspricht. (2) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht nutzen oder offenlegen, wer 1. das Geschäftsgeheimnis durch eine eigene Handlung nach Absatz 1 a) Nummer 1 oder b) Nummer 2 erlangt hat, 2. gegen eine Verpflichtung zur Beschränkung der Nutzung des Geschäftsgeheimnisses verstößt oder 3. gegen eine Verpflichtung verstößt, das Geschäftsgeheimnis nicht offenzulegen. (3) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangen, nutzen oder offenlegen, wer das Geschäftsgeheimnis über eine andere Person erlangt hat und zum Zeitpunkt der Erlangung, Nutzung oder Offenlegung weiß oder wissen müsste, dass diese das Geschäftsgeheimnis entgegen Absatz 2 genutzt oder offengelegt hat. Das gilt insbesondere, wenn die Nutzung in der Herstellung, dem Anbieten, dem Inverkehrbringen oder der Einfuhr, der Ausfuhr oder der Lagerung für diese Zwecke von rechtsverletzenden Produkten besteht. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall require' to 'request...to consider signing' directly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating an inconsistency that contradicts the German Trade Secrets Act. § 4 of the GeschGehG aims to protect trade secrets, and the perturbed text reduces the protection afforded by the NDA, which violates the original intent of the law. Requiring an NDA shows a reasonable measure to keep trade secrets confidential. Requesting that employees 'consider signing' weakens that measure.

perturbation.original_text: At Organiser ’s first request, Mentor shall: (a) discontinue all use of Confiden tial Information; (b) return to Organiser and/or Participants all materials furnished by Organiser and/or Participants that contain Confidential Information; (c) destroy any copy and all materials produced by and under control of Organiser and/or Partici pants that contain Confidential Information; (d) erase and/or destroy any Confidential Information contained in computer memory or data storage apparatus of, under control of or used by Mentor; (e) remove the Confidential Information from any software or data base of, under control of/or used by Mentor that incorporates or uses the Confidential Information in whole or in part; and (f) warrant in writing to Organiser, within ten (10) days after Organiser ’s request, that Mentor has taken all actions set ou t under (a) through (e) in this Clause 4.
perturbation.changed_text: At Organiser ’s first request, Mentor shall: (a) discontinue all use of Confiden tial Information; (b) return to Organiser and/or Participants all materials furnished by Organiser and/or Participants that contain Confidential Information; (c) destroy any copy and all materials produced by and under control of Organiser and/or Partici pants that contain Confidential Information; (d) erase and/or destroy any Confidential Information contained in computer memory or data storage apparatus of, under control of or used by Mentor; (e) remove the Confidential Information from any software or data base of, under control of/or used by Mentor that incorporates or uses the Confidential Information in whole or in part; and (f) warrant in writing to Organiser, within thirty (30) days after Organiser ’s request, that Mentor has taken all actions set ou t under (a) through (e) in this Clause 4.
perturbation.explanation: While seemingly minor, extending the deadline to warrant compliance with the return/destruction of confidential information from 10 to 30 days might not be sufficient to comply with certain German regulations depending on the sensitivity and volume of the data and could be seen as a breach of duty of care.
perturbation.contradicted_law: German Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG) & Art. 32 GDPR
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the contract is about confidential information and not specifically personal data, in the context of the GDPR, Article 32 requires implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including the deletion of the data. The BDSG complements the GDPR. In some cases, 10 days might be appropriate but depending on circumstances and amount of data, 30 days might expose to a higher risk and might conflict with the requirements for 'appropriate' measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the deadline for compliance regarding confidential information from 10 to 30 days. According to the Law Explanation, this extension "might expose to a higher risk and might conflict with the requirements for 'appropriate' measures" under GDPR Article 32, which requires implementing "appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including the deletion of the data." The Perturbation Explanation also supports that this change "might not be sufficient to comply with certain German regulations depending on the sensitivity and volume of the data and could be seen as a breach of duty of care." While the original text allowed 10 days, the change to 30 days introduces an Inconsistency that undermines the legally mandated standards for data security under GDPR.

perturbation.original_text: The place of juri sdiction for any and all legal disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement is Munich (Landgericht München I). Imperative places of jurisdiction under German Law shall remain unaffected by the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The place of juri sdiction for any and all legal disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement is Delaware, USA. Imperative places of jurisdiction under German Law shall remain unaffected by the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: Selecting Delaware, USA as the jurisdiction when the contract is clearly centered around a German entity and activities likely to occur in Europe is problematic. It ignores mandatory jurisdiction rules for employment-related contracts under EU law and potentially German law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Brussels I Regulation (Recast) - Regulation (EU) No 1215/2012 Article 21
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 21 and related articles of the Brussels I Regulation (Recast) specify jurisdiction rules for individual contracts of employment. Selecting Delaware likely violates the employee's right to sue in their country of residence or where the work is habitually carried out, rendering the jurisdiction clause potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the jurisdiction from Munich, Germany, to Delaware, USA. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change ignores mandatory jurisdiction rules for employment-related contracts under EU and potentially German law. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that Brussels I Regulation specifies jurisdiction rules for individual contracts of employment. Selecting Delaware likely violates the employee's right to sue in their country of residence, making the jurisdiction clause potentially unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Regulation (EU) No 1215/2012, indicating that the regulation exists, and it concerns jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters. The change introduces an Inconsistency that creates a Legal Contradiction because the chosen jurisdiction violates the employee's legal rights to sue where they reside or work, contradicting EU law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective as of 2 May 2017 and may not be terminated except for important cause.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective as of 2 May 2017 and may not be terminated by Mentor for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the mentor's ability to terminate the contract for any reason could be seen as overly restrictive and violate principles of German law regarding unfair contract terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 307 of the BGB prohibits unfair disadvantage in standard business terms. Completely restricting the Mentor's ability to terminate the contract, even in situations where it might be reasonable or necessary, could be considered an unfair disadvantage and render the clause unenforceable. Good cause would allow the mentor to end the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 307 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 307 Inhaltskontrolle (1) Bestimmungen in Allgemeinen Geschäftsbedingungen sind unwirksam, wenn sie den Vertragspartner des Verwenders entgegen den Geboten von Treu und Glauben unangemessen benachteiligen. Eine unangemessene Benachteiligung kann sich auch daraus ergeben, dass die Bestimmung nicht klar und verständlich ist. (2) Eine unangemessene Benachteiligung ist im Zweifel anzunehmen, wenn eine Bestimmung 1. mit wesentlichen Grundgedanken der gesetzlichen Regelung, von der abgewichen wird, nicht zu vereinbaren ist oder 2. wesentliche Rechte oder Pflichten, die sich aus der Natur des Vertrags ergeben, so einschränkt, dass die Erreichung des Vertragszwecks gefährdet ist. (3) Die Absätze 1 und 2 sowie die §§ 308 und 309 gelten nur für Bestimmungen in Allgemeinen Geschäftsbedingungen, durch die von Rechtsvorschriften abweichende oder diese ergänzende Regelungen vereinbart werden. Andere Bestimmungen können nach Absatz 1 Satz 2 in Verbindung mit Absatz 1 Satz 1 unwirksam sein. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Mentor's ability to terminate the contract for any reason, while the original allowed termination for 'important cause'. This creates an inconsistency that could be viewed as an unfair disadvantage under § 307 BGB. The scraped snippets define that terms in business conditions are invalid if they inappropriately disadvantage the other party against good faith. Removing any termination rights could be seen as inappropriately disadvantaging the Mentor, since the original allowed for termination based on 'important cause'.
